title: Filhol (2020): A Human-Editable Sign Language Representation . . . and a Writing System?
subtitle: A review of semantic-layer insight, SignWriting misclassification, and overstated novelty claims
summary: Review essay on Filhol's 2020 representation proposal.
synopsis: This review argues that Filhol is right about the limits of video and the importance of editable, queryable signed-language representation, but wrong to treat SignWriting as merely phonographic and structurally inadequate. The paper's durable value is as semantic-layer prompting, not as a successful reclassification of SignWriting.

slides.title: What Filhol Sees
slides.summary: Filhol is right that video is not writing and that editable, queryable signed-language representation matters.
slides.speech: This review begins by crediting Filhol's strongest insight. Video is not writing, and signed-language research needs editable, queryable, computationally usable representation. The paper also recognizes the importance of two-dimensional composition.

image_prompts.concrete: Review slide crediting Filhol for identifying the limits of video and the need for editable, queryable, computational signed-language representation
image_prompts.abstract: A useful computational insight separated from broader replacement claims

slides.title: Where The Claim Overreaches
slides.summary: The paper overreaches when it treats SignWriting as merely phonographic and structurally inadequate.
bullets: Solved technical problems are treated as unsolved
Semantic representation is treated as writing replacement
slides.speech: The review's critique is that Filhol overreaches. SignWriting is treated as merely phonographic and structurally inadequate, and a semantic-layer proposal begins to sound like a replacement for a writing system.

image_prompts.concrete: Critical review slide showing a semantic representation proposal overextended into a writing-system replacement claim
image_prompts.abstract: A useful layer stretching beyond its proper boundary

slides.title: Framework Reading
slides.summary: Through the framework, Filhol is valuable as semantic-layer prompting, not as a successful reclassification of SignWriting.
slides.speech: Through the framework, Filhol's durable value is semantic-layer prompting. It pressures the field to care about editability, queryability, and computation, but it does not successfully reclassify SignWriting or replace its writing-system account.
slides.notes: This slide preserves the value while naming the limit.

image_prompts.concrete: Layer-based reading slide separating writing need, alphabetic bias, structural unit analysis, spatial organization, and claims versus evidence
image_prompts.abstract: A source claim passed through a clear layer-based review frame

slides.title: Platform Relevance
slides.summary: The review clarifies why semantic representation, signed writing, and machine-readable text must remain separate layers.
bullets: Layer discipline prevents false replacement claims
slides.speech: The platform relevance is clear: semantic representation matters, but it is not the same layer as signed writing or machine-readable text. Keeping those layers separate prevents useful proposals from becoming false replacement claims.

image_prompts.concrete: Platform relevance slide separating semantic representation, signed writing, formal text models, and machine-readable infrastructure
image_prompts.abstract: Several related layers aligned without collapsing into one another
